claim: Bariatric surgery reduces resolution of diabetes.

result.claim: Bariatric surgery reduces resolution of diabetes.
result.reasoning:
The claim "Bariatric surgery reduces resolution of diabetes" is largely refuted by the available evidence. Multiple propositions indicate that bariatric surgery is effective in treating, ameliorating, and even curing diabetes. For example, paper ID 72120780 states that "Metabolic surgery is effective in treating diabetes" (prop_4752). Paper ID 9716341 asserts that "Bariatric surgery has been proven to ameliorate and even cure diabetes" (prop_2232) and that "Roux-en-Y gastric bypass is a procedure that can control diabetes mellitus" (prop_2233). Similarly, paper ID 30393433 highlights that "Bariatric surgery established itself as a cure to diabetes in a significant proportion of cases" (prop_4319) and is "the most effective means of treating diabetes in obese individuals" (prop_4318). The term "metabolic surgery" was coined due to the "excellent results of bariatric surgery in improving glucose homeostasis in obese patients with type 2 diabetes" (prop_4320, 30393433).

While one proposition (prop_2591) from paper ID 12200766 initially appeared to support the claim by stating "There was a significant decrease in the rate for diabetes resolution (P = 0.035)," the abstract of the same paper directly contradicts this by concluding, "Important resolution of comorbidities, such as hypertension and diabetes, was also seen." This internal inconsistency within paper 12200766 makes the specific proposition regarding a "decrease in the rate for diabetes resolution" unreliable and likely misinterpreted without further context. The overwhelming majority of other evidence points to bariatric surgery having a positive impact on diabetes resolution.

evidence.text: 28 LAGB participants experienced partial remission of diabetes at 3 years.
evidence.source: Weight Change and Health Outcomes at 3 Years After Bariatric Surgery Among Individuals With Severe Obesity

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery established itself as a cure to diabetes in a significant proportion of cases.
evidence.source: Revisiting the Role of BMI in the Guidelines for Bariatric Surgery.

evidence.text: Strong associations were found between bariatric surgery and the resolution of T2DM, with a HR of 9.29 (95% CI 6.84-12.62).
evidence.source: Bariatric Surgery in the United Kingdom: A Cohort Study of Weight Loss and Clinical Outcomes in Routine Clinical Care.

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery established itself as the most effective means of treating diabetes in obese individuals.
evidence.source: Revisiting the Role of BMI in the Guidelines for Bariatric Surgery.

evidence.text: The term “metabolic surgery” was coined due to the excellent results of bariatric surgery in improving glucose homeostasis in obese patients with type 2 diabetes.
evidence.source: Revisiting the Role of BMI in the Guidelines for Bariatric Surgery.

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery has been proven to ameliorate and even cure diabetes.
evidence.source: Surgical approaches for the prevention and treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus.

evidence.text: Adjustable gastric banding is a procedure that can control diabetes mellitus.
evidence.source: Surgical approaches for the prevention and treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus.

evidence.text: Metabolic surgery is effective in treating diabetes.
88.2% of patients had diabetes mellitus after the surgery.
evidence.source: OUTCOMES ON QUALITY OF LIFE, WEIGHT LOSS, AND COMORBIDITIES AFTER ROUX-EN-Y GASTRIC BYPASS

evidence.text: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass is a procedure that can control diabetes mellitus.
evidence.source: Surgical approaches for the prevention and treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus.

evidence.text: 88.2% of all patients who had the surgery had diabetes mellitus.
evidence.source: OUTCOMES ON QUALITY OF LIFE, WEIGHT LOSS, AND COMORBIDITIES AFTER ROUX-EN-Y GASTRIC BYPASS

evidence.text: After 3 years, 68.7% of RYGBP participants were in diabetes remission.
evidence.source: Type 2 Diabetes Remission Rates After Laparoscopic Gastric Bypass and Gastric Banding: Results of the Longitudinal Assessment of Bariatric Surgery Study

evidence.text: Bariatric surgery has a remarkable effect on glucose metabolism in morbidly obese and less obese patients.
Relative diabetes remission rates remained nearly twofold higher after RYGBP than LAGB, even after controlling for differences in amount of weight lost.
evidence.source: Type 2 Diabetes Remission Rates After Laparoscopic Gastric Bypass and Gastric Banding: Results of the Longitudinal Assessment of Bariatric Surgery Study

evidence.text: Bypass surgery had a cost-effectiveness ratio of $7,000 per QALY for severely obese patients with newly diagnosed diabetes.
evidence.source: Cost-Effectiveness of Bariatric Surgery for Severely Obese Adults With Diabetes

evidence.text: There was a significant decrease in the rate for diabetes resolution (P = 0.035).
evidence.source: OUTCOMES ON QUALITY OF LIFE, WEIGHT LOSS, AND COMORBIDITIES AFTER ROUX-EN-Y GASTRIC BYPASS

evidence.text: After 3 years, 30.2% of LAGB participants were in diabetes remission.
evidence.source: Type 2 Diabetes Remission Rates After Laparoscopic Gastric Bypass and Gastric Banding: Results of the Longitudinal Assessment of Bariatric Surgery Study
